subUnits.title: 3.1 Arabian Peninsula Before Islam - Geographic and Social Conditions
notes: - Syria and Iraq to the north
• Total area: approximately 3.2 million sq km
• Mostly desert with limited arable land
• Predominantly desert (Rub' al Khali - Empty Quarter)
• Extremely hot in summer, cold in winter
• Oases provided water and limited agriculture
• Inhabited by Aws and Khazraj tribes
4. Other cities: Khaybar, Fadak, Yemen cities
• Society organized into tribes and clans
• Treated as property in many cases
• Major idols: Hubal, Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, Manat
• Each tribe had its own idols
• Hanifs (few who followed Ibrahim's pure monotheism)
• Caravan routes to Syria and Yemen
• Summer and winter journeys (mentioned in Qur'an)
هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ
It is He who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from among themselves
Describes the condition of disbelief and corruption
• This created the need for divine guidance
• Arabia was in a state of Jahiliyyah (ignorance)
• Perfect time for Allah's final message
• Prophet Muhammad (SAW) came to transform this society
diagramDescriptions: Map of Arabian Peninsula showing major cities, trade routes, and tribes
Chart showing social structure: nobles, free Arabs, freed slaves, slaves
Diagram of Ka'bah surrounded by idols before Islam

Table comparing positive and negative aspects of pre-Islamic society

quiz.q: The Arabian Peninsula is bordered by all EXCEPT:
quiz.explanation: The Arabian Peninsula is bordered by the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, and Arabian Sea, but not the Mediterranean Sea.

quiz.q: The most important city in pre-Islamic Arabia was:
quiz.explanation: Makkah was the most important city due to the Ka'bah and its position as a trade center.

quiz.q: Pre-Islamic Arabian society was organized based on:
quiz.explanation: Arab society was organized into tribes and clans with strong tribal loyalty.

quiz.q: The number of idols around the Ka'bah was:
quiz.explanation: There were 360 idols around the Ka'bah before Islam.

quiz.q: Major idols worshipped included all EXCEPT:
quiz.explanation: Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, and Manat were major idols; Ar-Rahman is one of Allah's names.

quiz.q: The practice of burying baby girls alive was called:
quiz.explanation: Female infanticide was sadly practiced in pre-Islamic Arabia due to the low status of women.

quiz.q: Religions present in Arabia before Islam included:
quiz.explanation: Arabia had idol worship (majority), Judaism (in Yathrib/Khaybar), Christianity (in Najran), and a few Hanifs.

quiz.q: Makkah was controlled by which tribe?
quiz.explanation: The Quraysh tribe controlled Makkah and the Ka'bah before Islam.

quiz.q: A positive aspect of pre-Islamic Arab society was:
quiz.explanation: Despite many negatives, Arabs were known for their hospitality, courage, and eloquence.

quiz.q: The term 'Jahiliyyah' means:
quiz.explanation: Jahiliyyah means the age of ignorance, referring to the pre-Islamic period of religious and moral ignorance.

subUnits.title: 3.2 Religious and Economic Life of Pre-Islamic Arabs
notes: • Worship of multiple gods and idols
• Each tribe had its own idols
• Belief that idols could intercede with Allah
أَلَا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخَالِصُ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِن دُونِهِ أَوْلِيَاءَ
Unquestionably, for Allah is the pure religion. And those who take protectors besides Him say, 'We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah'
- Red agate stone in human form
Quran mentions these in Surah An-Najm (53:19-20):
Have you considered al-Lat and al-Uzza? And Manat, the third - the other one?
• Belief in omens and bad luck
• Hanifs: few who rejected idol worship
Quran mentions this in Surah Quraysh (106:1-2):
For the accustomed security of the Quraysh. Their accustomed security in the caravan of winter and summer
• Poor and weak had no protection
Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan
• Established justice in all aspects of life
diagramDescriptions: Chart showing major idols and their locations in Arabia
Map showing trade routes: summer route to Syria, winter route to Yemen
Diagram showing economic structure: rich merchants, poor, slaves
Comparison table: Pre-Islamic economic practices vs Islamic reforms
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Pre-Islamic Arabia religious economic life SSS1 IRS site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The chief idol in the Ka'bah was:
quiz.explanation: Hubal was the chief idol placed inside the Ka'bah before Islam.

quiz.q: The idols Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, and Manat are mentioned in Surah:
quiz.explanation: These three idols are mentioned in Surah An-Najm (53:19-20).

quiz.q: Trade caravans went to Syria in:
quiz.explanation: Caravans traveled to Syria in summer and to Yemen in winter, as mentioned in Surah Quraysh.

quiz.q: The practice of charging interest was called:
quiz.explanation: Riba (usury/interest) was widely practiced in pre-Islamic Arabia.

quiz.q: Hanifs were:
quiz.explanation: Hanifs were few individuals who rejected idol worship and followed the pure monotheism of Prophet Ibrahim.

quiz.q: The most important livestock for Arabs was:
quiz.explanation: Camels were the most important livestock for transportation, trade, and survival in the desert.

quiz.q: Gambling in pre-Islamic Arabia was done using:
quiz.explanation: Arrows were used for divination and gambling in pre-Islamic Arabia.

quiz.q: The market of Ukaz was famous for:
quiz.explanation: Ukaz was a famous market where trade and poetry competitions took place.

quiz.q: Women in pre-Islamic Arabia had:
quiz.explanation: Women had very limited rights and generally no inheritance rights in pre-Islamic Arabia.

quiz.q: Islam reformed the economic system by introducing:
quiz.explanation: Islam introduced Zakat (obligatory charity) and prohibited Riba (interest) to establish economic justice.
